coredata.dc:title: Construction of continuously enhanced fluorescent sensor for detection of glutathione in normal and cancer cells
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Erfei
Chen, Ting
Fang, Min
Zhu, Weiju
Li, Cun
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, water-soluble cysteamine (CA)-capping CdSe quantum dots (CA-CdSe) could be used as a continuous fluorescent sensor. The CA-CdSe QDs can respond to Ag+ with a detection limit of 54.1 nM. Interestingly, CA-CdSe quantum dots combined with Ag+ to generate a new nano-fluorescence sensor-Ag+ modified CA-CdSe QDs (Ag+@CA-CdSe). Ag+@CA-CdSe can detect glutathione (GSH) with good sensitivity and anti-interference performance. The detection limit of Ag+@CA-CdSe fluorescenct sensor for GSH is as low as 0.74 μM. In addition, the novel nano-fluorescent sensor Ag+@CA-CdSe exhibited good cell permeability and was successfully applied to detect exogenous and endogenous GSH concentrations in cells. It could distinguish cancerous and normal cells by in vitro cell fluorescence imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CA-CdSe quantum dots
Ag+@CA-CdSe
Ions detection
Glutathione
Continuous fluorescent sensor
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural characterization of CA-CdSe quantum dots
Detection of GSH using Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots
CA-CdSe quantum dots detection mechanism of Ag+ and GSH
Application and detection of GSH in cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD pattern of CA-CdSe quantum dots. (b) Infrared spectra of cysteamine hydrochloride (CA) and CA-CdSe quantum dots. (c) Transmission electron microscope image of CA-CdSe quantum dots. (d) High-resolution transmission electron microscope image of CA-CdSe quantum dots.

(a) The fluorescence emission spectra of CA-CdSe quantum dots after adding different metal ions (EX Slit: 10.0 nm, EM Slit: 10.0 nm, PMT Voltage: 400 V, λex = 350 nm). (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots after adding different amino acids (EX Slit: 5.0 nm, EM Slit: 5.0 nm, PMT Voltage: 400 V, λex = 350 nm). (c) Histogram of fluorescence intensity at the maximum emission wavelength after adding different amino acids to Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of different concentrations Ag+ added to CA-CdSe quantum dots (EX Slit: 5.0 nm, EM Slit: 5.0 nm, PMT Voltage: 700 V, λex = 350 nm). (b) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity enhancement value (F-F0) and Ag+ concentration of CA-CdSe quantum dots after adding different concentrations of Ag+. (c)Fluorescence emission spectra of different concentrations GSH added to Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots (EX Slit: 5.0 nm, EM Slit: 5.0 nm, PMT Voltage: 400 V, λex = 350 nm). (d) The linear relationship between F-F0 and the added concentration of GSH.
Competitive experimental selectivity of various amino acids in Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots (L1).

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectra of CA-CdSe quantum dots with Ag+ and GSH added. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of Ag+ and GSH added to CA-CdSe quantum dots with different orders. Inset: photograph of different samples under 365 nm UV lamp, from left to right: CA-CdSe, CA-CdSe + Ag+, CA-CdSe + Ag++GSH, CA-CdSe + GSH. (c)XRD pattern of CA-CdSe quantum dots after adding Ag+. (d) Infrared spectra of CA-CdSe quantum dots with Ag+, GSH and pure GSH.

Fluorescence confocal image. (a1) CA-CdSe quantum dots (10 μM) were incubated in Hela cells for 30 min. (a2) Ag+ was added to Hela cells incubated with CA-CdSe quantum dots and incubated for 10 min. (a3) Ag+ was added to Hela cells incubated with CA-CdSe quantum dots and then GSH was added to incubate for 10 min. (b1), (b2), (b3) brightfield; (c1), (c2), (c3) superposition of brightfield and darkfield.

Fluorescence confocal image. (a) CA-CdSe quantum dots (10 μM) were incubated in 293T cells and 3T3 cells, Ag+ and GSH was added to cells in turn. (b) A549 cells and Hela cells. Relative fluorescence intensities of (c) 293T cells, 3T3 cells and (d) A549 cells, Hela cells as used in panels (a) and (b). The error bars mean SD (n = 33 cells).
Schematic diagram of the mechanism of CA-CdSe quantum dots for Ag+ ion and glutathione (GSH) detection.
Comparison of various optical nanosensors for GSH detection.

head.ce:title: Construction of continuously enhanced fluorescent sensor for detection of glutathione in normal and cancer cells

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, water-soluble cysteamine (CA)-capping CdSe quantum dots (CA-CdSe) could be used as a continuous fluorescent sensor. The CA-CdSe QDs can respond to Ag+ with a detection limit of 54.1 nM. Interestingly, CA-CdSe quantum dots combined with Ag+ to generate a new nano-fluorescence sensor-Ag+ modified CA-CdSe QDs (Ag+@CA-CdSe). Ag+@CA-CdSe can detect glutathione (GSH) with good sensitivity and anti-interference performance. The detection limit of Ag+@CA-CdSe fluorescenct sensor for GSH is as low as 0.74 μM. In addition, the novel nano-fluorescent sensor Ag+@CA-CdSe exhibited good cell permeability and was successfully applied to detect exogenous and endogenous GSH concentrations in cells. It could distinguish cancerous and normal cells by in vitro cell fluorescence imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) is a tripeptide with sulfhydryl groups combined with glutamic acid, cysteine and glycine, which has anti-oxidant and integrated detoxification effects. Therefore, glutathione can participate in biotransformation, thereby converting harmful poisons in the body into harmless substances and excreting them out of the body. Glutathione can also help maintain the normal function of the immune system as an important role in physiological processes [1], among which the abnormal concentrations of thiol molecules are closely related to the formation of some important diseases such as cancer [2,3], AIDS, Alzheimer's disease, and cardiovascular disease [4–6]. Glutathione acts as a redox regulator [7] to scavenge free radicals in the body, so the detection of GSH is of great biological significance. At present, the reported GSH detection methods mainly include: enzymatic cycling [8], spectrophotometry [3], flow cytometry, high performance liquid chromatography [9], high performance capillary electrophoresis [10], electrochemical method [11], isotope method [12], mass spectrometry [13], fluorescence spectroscopy [14], etc. Some methods have disadvantages of low sensitivity, slow speed, poor selectivity, complicated process, and expensive experimental equipment. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a fast, high-sensitivity, and specific GSH sensor. Cao et al. [15] reported a new fluorescent sensor based on naphthalimide using sulfonamide units as sulfhydryl reactive groups, which can recognize glutathione, cysteine, homocysteine with high selectivity. It can display the level of glutathione in cell lysosomes. Jia et al. [16] reported a fluorescent sensor based on bifunctional vanadium oxide quantum dots with rich redox chemistry and strong fluorescence. In the presence of MnO4 −, VOx quantum dots spontaneously induced the formation of MnO2 nanosheets, which resulted in fluorescence quenching. However, the addition of glutathione can lead to the reduction of MnO2 to Mn2+ and the recovery of fluorescence. So an “on-off-on” fluorescent sensor method for glutathione detection was established. Qi et al. [17] reported a fluorescent sensor based on nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) as sensors for the detection of Hg2+ and glutathione (GSH). Hg2+ can effectively quench the fluorescence of N, S-CDs, while GSH can restore the fluorescence for the stronger bond between Hg2+ and the sulfhydryl of GSH. Sun et al. [18] reported the nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs)could use as continuous fluorescent sensors for Cu2+ and GSH. The quenched fluorescence of N, S-CDs@Cu2+could be recovered after adding GSH. It indicates that Cu2+ modified N, S-CDs is an efficient fluorescent sensor for GSH detection. So, QDs fluorescent sensor can furnish detection for metal ions and GSH, where the metal ion modified QDs could continuous detect GSH. In addition, compared with the previously reported literature, quantum dots alone to detect GSH [19] and detect metal ions-GSH [20], are not applied to cell imaging to detect GSH in normal cells and cancer cells, and it is also rare to use CdSe quantum dots and silver ions to study glutathione imaging in cells.

In the paper a continuous fluorescent sensor for Ag+ and GSH determination based on water-soluble CdSe quantum dots was investigated. Water-soluble cysteamine capping CdSe quantum dots (CA-CdSe) could be used as continuous fluorescent sensor mainly by the specific binding reaction mechanism of thiol and quantum dots. The combination of CA-CdSe quantum dots and Ag+ displays a fluorescence enhancement effect, and the Ag+ modified quantum dots (Ag+@CA-CdSe) can re-recognize GSH and enhance ulteriorly the fluorescence of quantum dots. In addition, Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots can be used as sensitive fluorescent sensor for GSH detection in normal and cancer cells through fluorescence imaging. The detection procedure of Ag+ ions and GSH by CA-CdSe quantum dots is shown in Scheme 1 .

Cadmium chloride hydrate (CdCl2·2.5H2O), Selenium powder (Se), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), dilute hydrochloric acid (HCl), cysteamine hydrochloride (CA), l-Cysteine(l-Cys), glutathione (GSH), arginine (Arg), aspartic acid (Asp), glycine (Gly), leucine (Leu), lysine (Lys), glutamic acid (Glu), Proline (Pro) and Homocystine (Hcy) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., ltd. (China). The metal ions of Ba2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Fe3+ are all nitrates, and the metal ion of Ni2+ is chloride. The reagents used in this study are all analytical reagent grades without further treatment.

X-ray diffractometer (9 kW / smartlab). High resolution transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100). UV visitor (UV-1800). Fluorescence spectrophotometer (F − 2500). Fourier infrared microscopy system (IR Prestige-21). X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi). Laser scanning confocal microscope (Zeiss LSM 710).

Water-soluble CA-CdSe quantum dots were synthesized by modified previously reported method [21]. Deionized water was bubbled with N2 for 40 min to remove dissolved oxygen. 0.4 mmol of selenium powder (Se) and 3.2 mmol of NaBH4 were put into a centrifuge tube containing 3 mL of deionized water to obtain a colorless transparent NaHSe solution. Separately, 0.8 mmol CdCl2·2.5H2O and 1.92 mmol cysteamine hydrochloride (CA) were added to a three-necked flask with 80 mL deionized water stirring for 30 min under N2 protective atmosphere. The NaHSe solution was quickly added into the CdCl2 solution, and the pH values adjusted to different value with 1 M NaOH solution. Then the mixture was refluxed 4 h at different temperatures under N2 atmosphere to obtain CA-CdSe quantum dots solution. The quantum dots were purified by precipitation with isopropanol, centrifuged, washed, dried and dispersed in ultrapure water for use. The influences of pH and temperature on CA-CdSe quantum dots were described in the Supplementary data. In brief, CA-CdSe quantum dots synthesized under the reaction conditions of pH = 5 (Fig. S1) and 40 °C (Fig. S2) for subsequent experiments.

The prepared CA-CdSe quantum dot diluted to concentration of 1.0 × 10−5 M. Different volumes of 1.0 × 10−2 M Ag+ ion solutions were added into 4 mL of quantum dot solution. Fluorescence detection with Ag+ concentration of 0–18.0 μM.

Detection of glutathione (GSH): 1.0 × 10−5 M Ag+ ions were added into CA-CdSe quantum dots solution to form functional Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots solutions. Then different concentration GSH solutions were added into above solution to carry out fluorescence measurement where the concentration of GSH ranged from 1.0 to 7.0 × 10−5 M.

Cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin (100 μg/mL), and streptomycin (100 μg/mL) at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere with 5 % CO2 and 95 % air.

A549, Hela cancer cells and 293 T, 3 T3 normal cells are seeded into a 35 mm Petri dish and cultured for 12 h. Before imaging, pretreatment with fresh medium, PBS solution washed three times, and then incubated with 5 μM CA-CdSe QDs solution for 30 min, and fluorescence images were obtained by Leica confocal microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of CA-CdSe quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD pattern of CA-CdSe quantum dots are shown in Fig. 1 a. It can be seen that there are 3 broad diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 25.2°, 42.6° and 49.4° respectively. They correspond to the three crystal planes of (111), (220), and (311) of face-centered cubic crystal structure CdSe (JCPDF No. 19-0191), which indicate that the CdSe quantum dots possess the cubic phase crystal structure. Fig. 1b displays the infrared spectra of cysteamine hydrochloride (CA) and CA-CdSe quantum dots. It can be found that cysteamine has a characteristic peak of thiol group (-SH) at 2519 cm−1, which disappears in the infrared spectrum of CA-CdSe quantum dots. It proves that the Cd on the surface of CA-CdSe to form a covalent bond by the cleavage of –SH bond in cysteamine [22]. Fig. 1c is a TEM image of CA-CdSe quantum dots. It can be seen that the CdSe quantum dots dispersed with the uniform size of particles about 3.41 nm. Fig. 1d shows a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image of the synthesized CA-CdSe quantum dots. The lattice fringe spacing calculated by the scale is 0.343 nm, which is consistent with the (111) crystal plane distance of the cubic phase structure CdSe. It further confirms that CdSe quantum dots have been successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GSH using Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that some metal ions have a great influence on the fluorescence intensity of QDs., The fluorescence emission spectra of CA-CdSe quantum dots after adding different metal ions (Ba2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Fe3+) are shown in Fig. 2 a. After adding the Ag+ ion solution to the quantum dots, the fluorescence of the quantum dots was greatly enhanced about 7 times, while the addition of other metal ions did not increase the fluorescence intensity of the quantum dots, and even some metal ions slightly weakened the fluorescence of the quantum dots. The above results show that CA-CdSe quantum dots have specific recognition for Ag+. In order to investigate the Ag+ modified CA-CdSe quantum dots (Ag+@CA-CdSe) interact with different amino acids. The fluorescence emission spectra of some different amino acids add into the Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots solution show in Fig. 2b. It is obvious that the fluorescence emission enhanced after addition of arginine (Arg), and glutathione (GSH), aspartic acid (Asp), glycine (Gly), leucine (Leu), lysine (Lys), l-Cysteine(l-Cys), glutamic acid (Glu), proline (Pro), homocystine (Hcy) with a concentration of 5.0 × 10−5 M. The fluorescence enhancement effect of GSH is particularly obvious among them. Fig. 2c shows a histogram of fluorescence intensity at the maximum emission wavelength after adding different amino acids to Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots. It can be seen that CA-CdSe quantum dots have almost no fluorescence. When adding Ag+ to CA-CdSe quantum dots, it is found that the fluorescence enhance, and then adding the same concentration of 5.0 × 10−5 M different amino acids in succession only the fluorescence intensity of adding GSH changes obviously. The fluorescence intensity of Ag+@CA-CdSe QDs enhances significantly more than 2.5 times, while the fluorescence intensity of Ag+@CA-CdSe QDs after other amino acids addition almost has no variation. This result indicates that the Ag+ functionalized CA-CdSe quantum dots have certain specificity and selectivity for the fluorescence detection of GSH in aqueous solution.

In order to investigate the anti-interference ability of Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dot for GSH fluorescence detection to other common biomolecules, some amino acids such as arginine (Arg), glutathione (GSH), aspartic acid (Asp), glycine (Gly), leucine (Leu), lysine (Lys), ascorbic acid (AA), glutamic acid (Glu), proline (Pro), homocysteine (Hcy) were chosen to conduct anti-interference experiments. As shown in Fig. 4 , it is found that the fluorescence intensity of the solution will be enhanced only after adding GSH. Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots have good ability to recognize GSH without other amino acids interference. The results indicate that Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots have good selectivity and certain anti-interference ability for GSH detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CA-CdSe quantum dots detection mechanism of Ag+ and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 a shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of Ag+ and GSH added to CA-CdSe quantum dots. It can be seen that after adding GSH to the Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots, the absorption edge of the quantum dots has a slight red shift, which indicates that the surface of the Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots may be combined with GSH small molecules to change its particle size. Fig. 5b is the fluorescence emission spectra of CA-CdSe quantum dots with Ag+ and GSH added in different orders. It can be seen from the figure that the fluorescence intensity of adding Ag+ and then GSH is stronger than that of adding GSH and then Ag+. It can be found that the fluorescence intensity has only a slight change after the addition of GSH in CA-CdSe quantum dots, which can be ignored but the fluorescence intensity increases by more than 13 times after the addition of Ag+, and then after the addition of GSH, the fluorescence intensity is increased by about 2.5 times again, and the phenomenon of continuous fluorescence enhancement occurs. This is consistent with the phenomenon shown in Fig. 5(b, inset), it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of CA-CdSe quantum did not change obviously after adding GSH alone. It shows that the interaction between glutathione (GSH) and Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots can produce prominent fluorescence enhancement effect. The possible reason is Ag+ and the surface of quantum dots is combined, and it is easier to combine with GSH after modifying the surface defects of quantum dots. Furthermore, the principle of reaction is investigated by XRD and IR. The XRD patterns are shown in Fig. 5c. The seven diffraction peaks at 25.2°, 30.4°, 33.5°, 36.0°, 40.3°, 42.7°, 44.9° are corresponding to (020), (102), (112), (013), (122), (113), (032) diffraction peaks of Ag2Se (JCPDF: 24-1041). This result is similar to the literature [28], indicating that Ag+ increases the fluorescence intensity of CA-CdSe QDs, because Ag+ combines with Se on the surface of CA-CdSe QDs to form Ag2Se, which reduces the surface defects of quantum dots. The IR spectra are shown in Fig. 5d. It can be seen that the sulfydryl characteristic peak of GSH at 2516 cm−1 has disappeared after added to Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots. This is because the thiol bond (-SH) on glutathione can interact with the surface of Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots to make the thiol bond (-SH) disappear.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application and detection of GSH in cells
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the application of quantum dots in cell imaging, we performed bioimaging experiments of Hela cells with CA-CdSe and Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots. The evaluation of the cytotoxicity of CA-CdSe quantum dots to Hela cells by MTT method was given in Fig. S3. When the concentration of CA-CdSe quantum dots reaches 30 μM, the cell survival rate is still about 84 % incubated with Hela cells for 24 h, which indicates that the cytotoxicity of CA-CdSe quantum dots is low. As shown in Fig. 6 , the Hela cells do not show any fluorescence in the green channel when CA-CdSe quantum dots are incubated with cells for 30 min. After silver ions addition, the green channel shows weak fluorescence, and the distribution of some quantum dots in cells can be seen. Moreover, after successive addition of Ag+ and GSH, the green fluorescence is significantly enhanced. It can be seen clearly that the Ag+ ions modified quantum dots distribute in cells after interaction with GSH. Observing the merged image of green channel and bright field, it can be seen that Ag+ and GSH are added successively, and the change of green channel fluorescence is continuously enhanced. The results indicate that Ag+@CA-CdSe fluorescent sensor can detect GSH in Hela cells, and may also track the changes of intracellular GSH.

Although glutathione plays an important role in oxidation and anti-cancer properties, overexpression of glutathione is often observed in cancer cells [29], which usually represents significant differences in GSH levels of cancerous and normal cells [30–32]. Therefore, we explored the application of Ag+@CA-CdSe fluorescent sensor in distinguishing cancerous cells and normal cells by endogenous GSH fluorescence imaging. 293T, 3T3 cells, and A549, Hela cells were selected as normal cell models and cancer cell models, respectively. Confocal laser scanning microscopy images of normal cells and cancer cells were shown in Fig. 7 . It can be found that the fluorescence intensity of endogenous GSH in cancer cells (Fig. 7b) is stronger than that in normal cells (Fig. 7a). This is because cancer cells contain higher level GSH than normal cells. Fig. 7c and d can more intuitively express the change in fluorescence intensity. Thus, it is concluded that the Ag+@CA-CdSe fluorescent sensor can detect not only exogenous GSH, but also endogenous GSH in different types of cells. It can distinguish cancer cells from normal cells through fluorescence imaging similar to some reported results [33–36]. Therefore, the Ag+@CA-CdSe fluorescent QDs can be considered as a biosensor for the diagnosis and clinical evaluation of GSH related diseases in future biomedicine.

Cysteamine (CA)-modified CdSe quantum dots (CA-CdSe) were synthesized by reflux method, and the fluorescence enhancement phenomenon occurred with Ag+, and the Ag+ modified quantum dots (Ag+@CA-CdSe) could be used as a continuous fluorescent sensor to detect aqueous solutions and cells glutathione (GSH). The study found that Ag+ can enhance the fluorescence intensity of CA-CdSe quantum dots, which is due to the formation of Ag2Se on the surface of CA-CdSe quantum dots, which modifies the surface defects of quantum dots. The detection limit was 5.41 × 10−8 M. The formation of functionalized Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots is more inclined to combine with sulfhydryl groups on GSH to recognize GSH molecules. The detection range of GSH is 1.0–7.0 × 10−5 M, and the detection limit is 0.741 μM. Therefore, Ag+@CA-CdSe quantum dots can be used as a novel fluorescent sensor for GSH detection with high sensitivity and selectivity. In addition, the fluorescent sensor can be used for the detection of exogenous and endogenous GSH in cells, which can distinguish normal and cancer cell. The results indicate that the quantum dot probe has broad application prospects in the detection of biological small molecules.
Erfei Dong: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Ting Chen: . Min Fang: . Weiju Zhu: Methodology. Cun Li: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21404001, 51472001), the Anhui Province University Natural Science Research Project (No. KJ2020A0014), the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (No. 1808085ME155).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.122064.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Construction of a selective fluorescent probe for GSH based on a chloro-functionalized coumarin-enone dye platform
Chem. Eur. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, X. Lv, J. Liu, Y.Q. Sun, W. Guo, Construction of a Selective Fluorescent Probe for GSH Based on a Chloro-Functionalized Coumarin-enone Dye Platform, Chem. Eur. J. 21 (2015) 4747-4754.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A standardized protocol for comparable analysis of GSH/GSSG by UHPLC-ESI-MSMS for human plasma
J. Chromatogr. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.S. Claeson, S. Gouveia-Figueira, H. Stenlund, A.I. Johansson, A standardized protocol for comparable analysis of GSH/GSSG by UHPLC-ESI-MSMS for human plasma, J. Chromatogr. B 1104 (2019) 67-72.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress on the development of glutathione (GSH) selective fluorescent and colorimetric probes
Coord. Chem. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Lee, J. Li, X. Zhou, J. Yin, J. Yoon, Recent progress on the development of glutathione (GSH) selective fluorescent and colorimetric probes, Coord. Chem. Rev. 366 (2018) 29-68.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Kinetic and thermodynamic aspects of cellular thiol-disulfide redox regulation
Antioxid. Redox Signal.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.S. Jensen, R.E. Hansen, J.R. Winther, Kinetic and Thermodynamic Aspects of Cellular Thiol-Disulfide Redox Regulation, Antioxid. Redox Signal. 11 (2009) 1047-1058.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fast-response, highly sensitive and specific organoselenium fluorescent probe for thiols and its application in bioimaging
Chem. Comm.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Tang, L. Yin, X. Wang, Z. Chen, L. Tong, K. Xu, A fast-response, highly sensitive and specific organoselenium fluorescent probe for thiols and its application in bioimaging, Chem. Comm. 35 (2009) 5293-5295.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Critical roles of intracellular thiols and calcium in parthenolide-induced apoptosis in human colorectal cancer cells
Cancer Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zhang, C.N. Ong, H.M. Shen, Critical roles of intracellular thiols and calcium in parthenolide-induced apoptosis in human colorectal cancer cells, Cancer Lett. 208 (2004) 143-153.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The rate of cellular hydrogen peroxide removal shows dependency on GSH: mathematical insight into in vivo H2O2 and GPx concentrations
Free Radic. Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.F. Ng, F.Q. Schafer, G.R. Buettner, V.G.J. Rodgers, The rate of cellular hydrogen peroxide removal shows dependency on GSH: Mathematical insight into in vivo H2O2 and GPx concentrations, Free Radic. Res. 41 (2007) 1201-1211.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Measurement of oxidized glutathione by enzymatic recycling coupled to bioluminescent detection
Anal. Biochem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Mourad, K.L. Min, J.P. Steghens, Measurement of oxidized glutathione by enzymatic recycling coupled to bioluminescent detection, Anal. Biochem. 283 (2000) 146-152.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid determination of glutathione status in fish liver using high-performance liquid chromatography and electrochemical detection
J. Chromatogr. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Rodriguez-Ariza, F. Toribio, J. Lopez-Barea, Rapid determination of glutathione status in fish liver using high-performance liquid chromatography and electrochemical detection, J. Chromatogr. B 656 (1994) 311-318.
